title: The Animator: Sprite Animation, State Machines & Blend Trees
html:
From frozen sprite to living character

Your platformer hero can already run, jump, and detect ground. But it is a statue that slides around. In this lesson you give it life with Unity's Animator system: animation clips, an AnimatorController state machine, transitions driven by parameters, and a blend tree for smooth multi-directional motion.

The big idea: gameplay code never plays animations directly. Instead, your movement script describes the character's state by setting parameters (speed, grounded, vertical velocity), and the Animator decides which clip to play. This decoupling is why the same controller can drive a player, an enemy, or an NPC.

Animation clips: the raw motion

An Animation clip (.anim) records property values over time. For 2D sprite animation, the recorded property is usually the Sprite field of a SpriteRenderer, with a keyframe per frame of your sprite sheet. Slice a sprite sheet in the Sprite Editor (Sprite Mode: Multiple), then select the resulting sub-sprites and drag them onto the scene object to auto-create a clip, or open the Animation window (Window > Animation > Animation) and record manually.

Tip.
Set the clip's Samples (frames per second) to control playback speed without changing your code. Pixel-art runs often feel right at 10-12 fps, not 60.

The AnimatorController state machine

An AnimatorController is a visual state machine. Each state plays one clip (or a blend tree). Transitions (arrows) move between states when their conditions on parameters are met. Create one via Assets > Create > Animator Controller, assign it to the Animator component on your character, and open the Animator window.

Add parameters in the top-left of the Animator window. Four types exist:

TypeUse it for
FloatContinuous values: speed, vertical velocity. Drives blend trees.
IntDiscrete enumerations: weapon index, character variant.
BoolOn/off states: isGrounded, isCrouching. Stays until changed.
TriggerOne-shot pulses: jump, attack. Auto-resets after consuming a transition.

Driving the Animator from code

Cache the Animator in Awake and push state every frame. Use parameter hashes instead of strings; Animator.StringToHash avoids per-frame string lookups and is the standard professional pattern.

PlayerAnimator.cs
// Reads movement state and tells the Animator what to play. using UnityEngine; [RequireComponent(typeof(Animator))] public class PlayerAnimator : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private Rigidbody2D rb; [SerializeField] private SpriteRenderer sprite; private Animator _anim; // Hash the names once at load time. private static readonly int Speed = Animator.StringToHash("Speed"); private static readonly int VertSpeed = Animator.StringToHash("VertSpeed"); private static readonly int Grounded = Animator.StringToHash("IsGrounded"); private static readonly int JumpTrig = Animator.StringToHash("Jump"); void Awake() => _anim = GetComponent<Animator>(); void Update() { float vx = rb.velocity.x; _anim.SetFloat(Speed, Mathf.Abs(vx)); _anim.SetFloat(VertSpeed, rb.velocity.y); _anim.SetBool(Grounded, GroundCheck.IsGrounded); // Flip the sprite to face movement direction. if (Mathf.Abs(vx) > 0.05f) sprite.flipX = vx < 0f; } // Called by your input/jump code, not every frame. public void PlayJump() => _anim.SetTrigger(JumpTrig); }

In the Animator: create an Idle state and a Run state. Add a transition Idle → Run with condition Speed > 0.1, and Run → Idle with Speed < 0.1. Add a Jump state reachable from Any State with condition Jump (the trigger). For falling, add a transition to a Fall state when VertSpeed < 0 and back to Idle when IsGrounded is true.

Lab.
Turn OFF Has Exit Time on gameplay transitions (Idle/Run/Jump). Exit Time forces the current clip to finish first, which makes controls feel laggy. Leave it ON only for animations that must complete, like a death or attack flourish.

Blend trees: smooth, parameter-driven blends

A Blend Tree is a special state that mixes several clips based on a float. Right-click in the Animator > Create State > From New Blend Tree. Double-click it, set the blend parameter to Speed, and add motions: Idle at threshold 0, Walk at 3, Run at 7. Unity cross-fades them as Speed changes, giving you a continuous gait from one parameter.

2D blend trees (type 2D Simple Directional or 2D Freeform) take two parameters, perfect for top-down characters that aim in any direction. You will reuse this exact idea in the twin-stick and top-down lessons, where MoveX and MoveY blend an 8-direction sprite set.

Animation Events: sync code to frames

Select a clip, open the Animation window, and add an Animation Event on a specific frame. It calls a public method on any component on the same GameObject. Use it to spawn a footstep dust particle on the frame the foot lands, or to enable a hitbox on the frame a sword swings. This frame-accurate hook is essential for fighting and action games later.

🎮 Build This: Side-scrolling Platformer — Animated Hero

This extends the 2D Platformer from the previous lessons (tilemap level + ground-checked movement). You will give the existing hero a full animation set.

  1. Import a hero sprite sheet, slice it in the Sprite Editor, and create Idle, Run, Jump, and Fall clips. Tune Samples for feel.
  2. Create an AnimatorController, add it to the hero's Animator, and add parameters: Speed (float), VertSpeed (float), IsGrounded (bool), Jump (trigger).
  3. Wire PlayerAnimator.cs to set those parameters from the Rigidbody2D, and call PlayJump() from your existing jump input.
  4. Build transitions (Idle↔Run, Any→Jump, →Fall→Idle) and disable Has Exit Time on the gameplay ones.
  5. Replace Idle/Run with a Blend Tree on Speed for a smooth walk-to-run, and add an Animation Event on the run clip that spawns a footstep particle.

title: Cinemachine: Follow Cameras, Confiners & Screen Shake
html:
Stop hand-coding cameras

A good camera is invisible: it keeps the player framed, never jitters, never shows past the level edge, and punches with impact on a hit. Writing all that yourself in LateUpdate is fiddly. Cinemachine is Unity's official camera package that handles following, damping, dead zones, boundaries, and shake declaratively. You set rules; it produces smooth motion.

Install it via Window > Package Manager > Cinemachine. The model: keep one regular Camera tagged MainCamera, then add lightweight Virtual Cameras (vcams). A CinemachineBrain on the main camera blends between whichever vcam has the highest priority. You never move the real camera; you switch and configure vcams.

A 2D follow camera

From the menu, add a Cinemachine > 2D Camera (this gives a vcam with the framing transposer suited to orthographic). In the vcam's inspector:

  • Follow: drag your player Transform here. The vcam tracks it.
  • Body > Dead Zone: a central rectangle where the player can move without the camera reacting. Widen it so small jumps do not jerk the view.
  • Soft Zone & Damping: how slowly the camera catches up when the player leaves the dead zone. Higher damping = smoother but laggier.
  • Screen X / Screen Y: where on screen the target sits. Push Y up so the player sees more of what is below.
Tip.
For a 3D third-person or FPS camera you would instead use a vcam with 3rd Person Follow or POV aim. The same priority/blend model applies, which is why mastering it now pays off in the 3D shooter and racing lessons.

Confiners: never show past the level

To stop the camera revealing black void at a level's edge, add a Cinemachine Confiner 2D extension to the vcam. Give it a Bounding Shape 2D: a PolygonCollider2D (set to Is Trigger) drawn around the playable area. The confiner clamps the camera so its frustum stays inside that shape. Press Bake Collider after editing the shape so the cached path updates.

Screen shake with Impulse

Cinemachine shake uses an Impulse system: emitters broadcast a signal, listeners on vcams react. This is decoupled and reusable. Add a CinemachineImpulseListener extension to your vcam. Then, on objects that should shake the screen (an explosion prefab, the player on landing), add a CinemachineImpulseSource and fire it from code.

ScreenShake.cs
// One emitter you can fire from anywhere for camera shake. using UnityEngine; using Cinemachine; public class ScreenShake : MonoBehaviour { public static ScreenShake Instance { get; private set; } [SerializeField] private CinemachineImpulseSource source; void Awake() { Instance = this; if (source == null) source = GetComponent<CinemachineImpulseSource>(); } // force scales the shake: small hit vs. big explosion. public void Shake(float force = 1f) { source.GenerateImpulseWithForce(force); } }

Now any system can call ScreenShake.Instance.Shake(2f) on impact. The vcam's listener does the rest, and the Impulse Definition (decay, duration, shape) on the source controls how the punch feels. Pair a strong landing shake with the footstep particle from the last lesson and the world suddenly feels weighty.

Lab.
Add a second vcam over a secret room with higher Priority and a tighter orthographic size. Walk into a trigger, set its priority above the main vcam in code (vcam.Priority = 20), and watch the Brain smoothly blend from the gameplay shot to the close-up. Reverse priority on exit. You just made a cutscene-style camera with zero manual lerping.

Why this scales to 3D

The same brain/vcam/priority/blend/impulse architecture powers AAA 3D games. In the FPS lesson you will use a POV vcam parented to the player; in third-person you will use the 3rd Person Follow rig with collision avoidance; in racing you will blend between chase and cockpit vcams. Learn the pattern once here in 2D, reuse it everywhere.

🎮 Build This: Side-scrolling Platformer — Pro Camera Pass

This extends the 2D Platformer (now with an animated hero). You will replace any hard-coded camera with a polished Cinemachine rig.

  1. Install Cinemachine. Add a 2D vcam, set Follow to the hero, and tune the dead zone, damping, and screen position until movement feels smooth.
  2. Draw a PolygonCollider2D around your level, add a Confiner 2D extension, assign the shape, and bake it so the camera never shows past the edges.
  3. Add a CinemachineImpulseListener to the vcam and create the ScreenShake singleton with an ImpulseSource.
  4. Fire ScreenShake.Instance.Shake() when the hero lands a jump and when an enemy is stomped; tune the Impulse Definition for snappy feel.
  5. Add a second high-priority vcam triggered when entering a special area, and confirm the Brain blends smoothly in and out.

title: ScriptableObjects: Data-Driven Design & Event Channels
html:
Data that lives outside the scene

So far your data has lived on MonoBehaviours inside scenes and prefabs. That couples data to objects and forces duplication. A ScriptableObject (SO) is a data container that is a project asset, independent of any GameObject. One SO asset can be referenced by many objects, edited in the Inspector, and shipped with your build. This is the backbone of data-driven design: designers tune assets, programmers write systems that read them.

We introduce a new genre here: the Metroidvania — a large interconnected world where the player gains abilities (double jump, dash, wall-cling) that unlock new areas. It is the perfect showcase for SOs because abilities, enemy stats, and items are all data that many systems share.

Creating a ScriptableObject

Inherit from ScriptableObject and add a CreateAssetMenu attribute so you can make asset instances from the Assets menu. SOs use serialized fields just like MonoBehaviours, but they have no Update loop and no Transform — they are pure data plus optional methods.

EnemyStats.cs
// A reusable stat block. Create many assets: Bat, Slime, Knight... using UnityEngine; [CreateAssetMenu(fileName = "EnemyStats", menuName = "Game/Enemy Stats")] public class EnemyStats : ScriptableObject { public string displayName; public int maxHealth = 10; public float moveSpeed = 2f; public int contactDamage = 1; public Sprite icon; }

Now create Assets > Create > Game > Enemy Stats several times to make Bat, Slime, and Knight assets. An enemy prefab references one asset:

Enemy.cs
using UnityEngine; public class Enemy : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private EnemyStats stats; // drag a SO asset here private int _health; void Awake() => _health = stats.maxHealth; void Update() { transform.Translate(Vector2.left * stats.moveSpeed * Time.deltaTime); } }
Watch out.
SO fields are shared across everyone referencing the asset, and edits at runtime persist in the Editor (they reset on play in builds). Never write per-instance runtime state (like current health) into the SO. Read maxHealth from the SO, but store the live _health on the MonoBehaviour, as above.

SOs as event channels: decoupling systems

The most powerful SO pattern is the event channel. Instead of system A holding a direct reference to system B, both reference a shared SO asset. A raises the event on the asset; B listens to the same asset. Neither knows the other exists. This is how you keep a Metroidvania's many systems (HUD, audio, doors, achievements) from turning into spaghetti.

VoidEventChannel.cs
using System; using UnityEngine; [CreateAssetMenu(menuName = "Events/Void Event")] public class VoidEventChannel : ScriptableObject { // Listeners subscribe; raisers invoke. No direct references. public event Action OnRaised; public void Raise() => OnRaised?.Invoke(); }
Door.cs & BossDefeated.cs
// Raiser: the boss fires the shared channel when it dies. public class BossDefeated : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private VoidEventChannel bossDefeatedEvent; public void Die() => bossDefeatedEvent.Raise(); } // Listener: a faraway door opens, with zero reference to the boss. public class Door : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private VoidEventChannel bossDefeatedEvent; void OnEnable() => bossDefeatedEvent.OnRaised += Open; void OnDisable() => bossDefeatedEvent.OnRaised -= Open; private void Open() => gameObject.SetActive(false); }

Both components point at the same BossDefeated.asset event channel in the Inspector. Kill the boss in one room, a gate opens in another, and you wrote no glue connecting them. Always unsubscribe in OnDisable to avoid leaks and calls into destroyed objects.

Tip.
Generic event channels carry payloads: an IntEventChannel for damage amounts, a Vector2EventChannel for hit positions. Build one per data type you need and reuse them across every project.

Ability data as SOs

Represent each Metroidvania ability as an SO (AbilitySO with name, icon, an isUnlocked flag stored elsewhere, and a cooldown). The player holds a list of references; the HUD reads the same assets to draw the ability bar. Picking up an ability raises an event channel that the HUD listens to. Data, systems, and UI stay independent.

🎮 Build This: Metroidvania — Data-Driven Foundation

This starts a new genre: the Metroidvania. You will lay its data-driven backbone now and add combat and abilities next lesson.

  1. Create an EnemyStats SO and author three enemy assets (Bat, Slime, Knight). Make one enemy prefab whose behavior reads its stats from an assigned SO.
  2. Create an AbilitySO for Double Jump and Dash, each with a name and icon.
  3. Build a VoidEventChannel and a generic IntEventChannel (copy the pattern with an Action<int>).
  4. Make an ability pickup that, on collection, raises a channel; have a placeholder HUD listener log that the ability unlocked.
  5. Wire a boss-defeated event channel so killing a test enemy opens a door in a different part of the level, with no direct reference between them.

title: C# in Depth: Delegates/Events, Interfaces & Coroutines
html:
The C# that makes systems flexible

Your Metroidvania needs combat: things that take damage, abilities that fire over time, and systems that react when health changes. Three C# tools make this clean and reusable: delegates/events for broadcasting, interfaces for shared contracts, and coroutines for time-based logic. Master these and your code stops being a tangle of references.

Interfaces: a contract anything can fulfill

An interface defines methods a class promises to implement, without saying how. The classic game example is IDamageable: the player, enemies, breakable crates, and bosses can all be damaged, but each responds differently. Your weapon code does not care what it hits — only that it is damageable.

IDamageable.cs
public interface IDamageable { void TakeDamage(int amount, Vector2 hitFrom); bool IsAlive { get; } }
Health.cs
using System; using UnityEngine; public class Health : MonoBehaviour, IDamageable { [SerializeField] private int maxHealth = 5; private int _current; // C# event: anyone can react to health changes without us knowing them. public event Action<int, int> OnHealthChanged; // (current, max) public event Action OnDied; public bool IsAlive => _current > 0; void Awake() { _current = maxHealth; OnHealthChanged?.Invoke(_current, maxHealth); } public void TakeDamage(int amount, Vector2 hitFrom) { if (!IsAlive) return; _current = Mathf.Max(0, _current - amount); OnHealthChanged?.Invoke(_current, maxHealth); if (_current == 0) OnDied?.Invoke(); } }

Now any attacker hits via the interface. GetComponent<IDamageable>() returns the component if it implements the contract, regardless of its concrete type:

MeleeHitbox.cs
using UnityEngine; public class MeleeHitbox : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private int damage = 2; void OnTriggerEnter2D(Collider2D other) { // Works on enemies, crates, bosses, anything IDamageable. if (other.TryGetComponent<IDamageable>(out var target) && target.IsAlive) target.TakeDamage(damage, transform.position); } }
Tip.
Interfaces let you write one system that handles dozens of unrelated types. They are how professional codebases stay open to new content: add a new IDamageable and every weapon already supports it.

Delegates and events: broadcast, do not poll

A delegate is a typed reference to a method; an event is a delegate that only the owning class can invoke. Above, OnHealthChanged lets the HUD update without the Health script ever referencing the HUD. Compare this to the SO event channels from last lesson: C# events are great within a known object graph (HUD listening to the local player's Health), while SO channels shine for cross-scene, designer-wired events. Use both.

HealthBarHUD.cs
using UnityEngine; using UnityEngine.UI; public class HealthBarHUD : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private Health playerHealth; [SerializeField] private Slider bar; void OnEnable() => playerHealth.OnHealthChanged += Refresh; void OnDisable() => playerHealth.OnHealthChanged -= Refresh; private void Refresh(int current, int max) => bar.value = (float)current / max; }

Coroutines: logic that unfolds over time

A coroutine is a method that can pause and resume across frames using yield return. It runs on the main thread (no threading dangers) and is perfect for timed gameplay: dashes, invincibility frames, cooldowns, attack windups. It returns IEnumerator and is launched with StartCoroutine.

DashAbility.cs
using System.Collections; using UnityEngine; public class DashAbility : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private Rigidbody2D rb; [SerializeField] private float dashSpeed = 18f; [SerializeField] private float dashTime = 0.15f; [SerializeField] private float cooldown = 0.6f; private bool _onCooldown; public void TryDash(float dir) { if (!_onCooldown) StartCoroutine(DashRoutine(dir)); } private IEnumerator DashRoutine(float dir) { _onCooldown = true; float originalGravity = rb.gravityScale; rb.gravityScale = 0f; // float during dash rb.velocity = new Vector2(dir * dashSpeed, 0f); yield return new WaitForSeconds(dashTime); // pause this method rb.gravityScale = originalGravity; // resume here next frame yield return new WaitForSeconds(cooldown); _onCooldown = false; } }
Watch out.
Coroutines stop if the GameObject is disabled, and WaitForSeconds obeys Time.timeScale (it pauses when the game is paused) — use WaitForSecondsRealtime for UI that must run while paused. For per-frame movement inside a coroutine, prefer yield return null in a loop and use WaitForFixedUpdate when touching physics.

Putting it together: invincibility frames

Subscribe a coroutine-driven flash to OnHealthChanged: when damaged, start a coroutine that blinks the sprite and ignores further hits for half a second. Interfaces let the weapon hit anything; events let the HUD and i-frame system react; coroutines schedule the timing. That is a complete, decoupled combat loop.

🎮 Build This: Metroidvania — Combat & Abilities

This extends the Metroidvania from the previous lesson, adding a real combat and ability layer on its data-driven foundation.

  1. Add an IDamageable interface and a Health component (with OnHealthChanged and OnDied events) to the player and all three enemy types.
  2. Build a MeleeHitbox that damages anything implementing IDamageable via TryGetComponent.
  3. Make a HealthBarHUD that subscribes to the player's OnHealthChanged event and updates a uGUI Slider.
  4. Implement the Dash ability as a coroutine with windup, active frames, and cooldown; gate it behind the AbilitySO unlock from last lesson.
  5. Add a coroutine-based invincibility-frame flash triggered from OnHealthChanged so the player cannot be combo-killed.

title: UI with uGUI: Canvas, Anchors, HUD, Menus & UI Toolkit Intro
html:
Every game needs a face

HUDs, menus, dialogue boxes, inventories — players touch your UI constantly. Unity's main UI system is uGUI (the Canvas-based system): GameObjects with RectTransform and components like Image, Text (TextMeshPro), and Button. We introduce a new genre, the top-down adventure (a Zelda-like overworld), and give it a hearts HUD and a pause menu.

The Canvas and Render Modes

All UI lives under a Canvas. Add one via GameObject > UI > Canvas; Unity also creates an EventSystem that routes input to UI. The Canvas Render Mode matters:

ModeUse
Screen Space - OverlayHUDs/menus drawn on top of everything. Most common.
Screen Space - CameraUI rendered by a camera; allows perspective and effects.
World SpaceUI placed in the 3D/2D world: floating name tags, damage numbers.

RectTransform and anchors: the key to responsive UI

UI objects use RectTransform instead of Transform. The crucial concept is anchors: four handles that pin an element relative to its parent. Anchor to the top-left corner and the element stays in that corner on any screen size; anchor to all four corners (stretch) and it resizes with the parent. Get anchors right and your UI works on phones and ultrawide monitors alike.

Tip.
Add a Canvas Scaler to the Canvas, set UI Scale Mode to Scale With Screen Size, and pick a reference resolution (e.g. 1920x1080). Combined with proper anchors, this is the standard recipe for resolution-independent UI.

For automatic arrangement use layout groups: a HorizontalLayoutGroup on a hearts container auto-spaces heart icons; a VerticalLayoutGroup stacks menu buttons. Add a ContentSizeFitter to make a panel grow to fit its children.

Building a hearts HUD

The hearts HUD listens to the player's Health.OnHealthChanged event from last lesson and toggles heart sprites. Pure data-in, visuals-out.

HeartsHUD.cs
using UnityEngine; using UnityEngine.UI; public class HeartsHUD : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private Health playerHealth; [SerializeField] private Image[] hearts; // children in a layout group [SerializeField] private Sprite full, empty; void OnEnable() => playerHealth.OnHealthChanged += Redraw; void OnDisable() => playerHealth.OnHealthChanged -= Redraw; private void Redraw(int current, int max) { for (int i = 0; i < hearts.Length; i++) hearts[i].sprite = i < current ? full : empty; } }

A pause menu with buttons

Buttons expose an onClick event you can wire in the Inspector or in code. A pause menu typically toggles a panel and freezes the game with Time.timeScale = 0.

PauseMenu.cs
using UnityEngine; using UnityEngine.SceneManagement; public class PauseMenu : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private GameObject panel; private bool _paused; public void Toggle() { _paused = !_paused; panel.SetActive(_paused); Time.timeScale = _paused ? 0f : 1f; // freeze/unfreeze gameplay } public void Resume() => Toggle(); public void Restart() { Time.timeScale = 1f; SceneManager.LoadScene(SceneManager.GetActiveScene().buildIndex); } public void Quit() => Application.Quit(); }

Wire the Resume/Restart/Quit buttons' onClick to these methods in the Inspector. Hook the Escape key to Toggle() through the Input System.

Watch out.
When Time.timeScale = 0, anything using Time.deltaTime stops, including some animations and coroutines that use WaitForSeconds. UI animations and menu coroutines must use Time.unscaledDeltaTime / WaitForSecondsRealtime so the menu still responds while paused.

TextMeshPro and world-space UI

Use TextMeshPro (TMP) for all text — it is sharper and far more flexible than legacy Text. For floating damage numbers in your top-down adventure, put a small TMP on a World Space canvas above the enemy and animate it upward on hit.

UI Toolkit: the data-driven future

Unity also ships UI Toolkit, a retained-mode system inspired by web tech: layouts in UXML (markup) and styling in USS (CSS-like), bound to data in C#. It excels at complex, data-heavy editors and menus and reduces per-element GameObject overhead. uGUI remains the pragmatic default for world-space and physics-aware HUDs, but knowing UI Toolkit exists prepares you for tool building and large menu systems. A minimal USS rule reads like web CSS:

MainMenu.uss
.menu-button { width: 240px; height: 48px; margin: 6px; background-color: rgb(40, 44, 60); color: white; } .menu-button:hover { background-color: rgb(70, 78, 110); }
🎮 Build This: Top-down Adventure — HUD & Menus

This starts a new genre: the top-down adventure (Zelda-like). Reuse the Health + events system from the Metroidvania lessons; you will add save persistence to this same game next.

  1. Make a top-down player that moves on the X/Y plane (reuse the Input System and a blend-tree animator for 4/8-direction sprites).
  2. Add a Screen Space - Overlay Canvas with a Canvas Scaler set to Scale With Screen Size and a hearts container using a HorizontalLayoutGroup.
  3. Wire HeartsHUD to the player's Health.OnHealthChanged event so hearts deplete and refill correctly.
  4. Build a pause menu panel with Resume/Restart/Quit buttons using PauseMenu, toggled by Escape, with proper timeScale handling.
  5. Add a World Space TMP damage number that pops above enemies when hit, and verify anchoring by testing several aspect ratios.

title: Save Systems: Serialization, JSON, PlayerPrefs & Persistence
html:
Progress that survives quitting

A top-down adventure with no save is a tech demo. Players expect their hearts, collected items, opened chests, and position to persist. In this lesson you build a robust save/load architecture: PlayerPrefs for tiny settings, JSON serialization for structured game state, and a clean separation between data and the systems that produce it.

PlayerPrefs: for small, non-critical values

PlayerPrefs is a simple key-value store (ints, floats, strings) backed by the platform's registry/preferences. It is perfect for settings — volume, resolution, last-selected character — but not for save games: it is unstructured, easy to corrupt, and trivial to cheat.

Settings.cs
using UnityEngine; public static class Settings { public static float MasterVolume { get => PlayerPrefs.GetFloat("master_volume", 0.8f); set { PlayerPrefs.SetFloat("master_volume", value); PlayerPrefs.Save(); } } }

JSON serialization for real save files

For actual progress, define a plain serializable data class and convert it to JSON with JsonUtility. The class must be marked [System.Serializable] and use public fields (or [SerializeField] private ones); JsonUtility ignores properties.

SaveData.cs
using System; using System.Collections.Generic; using UnityEngine; [Serializable] public class SaveData { public int saveVersion = 1; // future-proofing, see below public int currentHealth = 5; public Vector3 playerPosition; public string currentScene; public List<string> openedChests = new(); // unique chest IDs public List<string> unlockedAbilities = new(); }
Tip.
JsonUtility is fast and serializes Unity types like Vector3, but it does not handle dictionaries or polymorphism. For those, use lists of serializable structs (a list of id/value pairs) or a library like Newtonsoft Json.NET (available via Package Manager).

Writing and reading the file

Save to Application.persistentDataPath — the correct, writable, platform-specific location on desktop, mobile, and WebGL. Never write to the project or Resources folder; those are read-only in builds.

SaveSystem.cs
using System.IO; using UnityEngine; public static class SaveSystem { private static string Path => System.IO.Path.Combine(Application.persistentDataPath, "save.json"); public static void Save(SaveData data) { string json = JsonUtility.ToJson(data, prettyPrint: true); // Write to a temp file then move: avoids corruption on crash mid-write. string tmp = Path + ".tmp"; File.WriteAllText(tmp, json); if (File.Exists(Path)) File.Delete(Path); File.Move(tmp, Path); } public static SaveData Load() { if (!File.Exists(Path)) return new SaveData(); // fresh game string json = File.ReadAllText(Path); return JsonUtility.FromJson<SaveData>(json); } public static bool HasSave() => File.Exists(Path); public static void Delete() { if (File.Exists(Path)) File.Delete(Path); } }
Watch out.
Writing directly over the existing file means a crash mid-write destroys the save. The write-temp-then-rename pattern above (atomic on most filesystems) keeps the last good save intact if anything fails.

Architecture: who owns the data?

Do not scatter SaveSystem.Save() calls everywhere. Use a central SaveManager that gathers state from systems and applies it back. A common, scalable pattern is an ISaveable interface: each system that has persistent state implements CaptureState and RestoreState, and the manager iterates them. This mirrors the interface decoupling you learned in the combat lesson.

SaveManager.cs
using UnityEngine; public class SaveManager : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private Health playerHealth; [SerializeField] private Transform player; [SerializeField] private ChestRegistry chests; public void SaveGame() { var data = new SaveData { currentHealth = playerHealth.Current, playerPosition = player.position, currentScene = UnityEngine.SceneManagement.SceneManager.GetActiveScene().name, openedChests = chests.GetOpenedIds() }; SaveSystem.Save(data); } public void LoadGame() { var data = SaveSystem.Load(); if (data.saveVersion != 1) data = Migrate(data); // handle old saves playerHealth.SetHealth(data.currentHealth); player.position = data.playerPosition; chests.RestoreOpened(data.openedChests); } private SaveData Migrate(SaveData old) => old; // upgrade fields across versions }

Versioning and unique IDs

Two production concerns: First, store a saveVersion so when you add fields in an update you can migrate old saves instead of crashing on them. Second, every persistent world object (chest, door, picked-up item) needs a stable unique ID string so the save can record exactly which ones changed. A small component that generates a GUID once in the Editor and serializes it is the standard solution. When loading, each object checks the save list for its ID and sets its state.

Law.
Save data, not objects. Your save file should be a flat, serializable description of state that can rebuild the world — never a snapshot of live GameObjects. This keeps saves small, portable across versions, and engine-independent.
🎮 Build This: Top-down Adventure — Save & Load

This extends the Top-down Adventure from the previous lesson, adding full persistence to its HUD-driven world.

  1. Define a SaveData class with health, position, current scene, opened chests, and unlocked abilities, plus a saveVersion.
  2. Implement SaveSystem with the atomic write-temp-then-rename pattern, saving to Application.persistentDataPath.
  3. Give chests and pickups a unique ID component and a ChestRegistry that tracks opened IDs.
  4. Build a SaveManager that captures and restores all state; wire Save to a menu button and auto-load on launch if HasSave().
  5. Store master volume in PlayerPrefs via the Settings class, and confirm progress survives quitting and relaunching the game.

title: Twin-Stick Shooter: Aiming, Pooling & Bullet Management
html:
Move with one stick, aim with the other

The twin-stick shooter is a new genre and a perfect lab for two skills: independent move/aim controls and object pooling to fire hundreds of bullets without the garbage-collection hitches that kill performance. Think Geometry Wars or Enter the Gungeon: the left stick moves, the right stick (or mouse) aims, and the screen fills with projectiles.

Twin-stick input

Using the Input System (from the Asteroids lesson), define two Vector2 actions: Move (WASD / left stick) and Aim (right stick) plus a mouse path. Mouse aim is a position you subtract the player's screen position from; gamepad aim is already a direction vector.

TwinStickController.cs
using UnityEngine; using UnityEngine.InputSystem; public class TwinStickController : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private float moveSpeed = 6f; [SerializeField] private Transform muzzle; [SerializeField] private float fireRate = 12f; // shots per second [SerializeField] private BulletPool pool; private Vector2 _move, _aim; private float _aimAngle, _fireTimer; private Rigidbody2D _rb; void Awake() => _rb = GetComponent<Rigidbody2D>(); public void OnMove(InputAction.CallbackContext c) => _move = c.ReadValue<Vector2>(); public void OnAim(InputAction.CallbackContext c) { Vector2 v = c.ReadValue<Vector2>(); if (v.sqrMagnitude > 0.04f) // deadzone _aimAngle = Mathf.Atan2(v.y, v.x) * Mathf.Rad2Deg; } void FixedUpdate() => _rb.velocity = _move * moveSpeed; void Update() { transform.rotation = Quaternion.Euler(0, 0, _aimAngle); // face aim dir _fireTimer -= Time.deltaTime; if (_fireTimer <= 0f) { Fire(); _fireTimer = 1f / fireRate; } } private void Fire() { Bullet b = pool.Get(); // reuse, never Instantiate b.transform.SetPositionAndRotation(muzzle.position, transform.rotation); b.Launch(transform.right); // right = facing direction } }

Why object pooling matters

Instantiate and Destroy are expensive: they allocate memory and create garbage that the GC must later collect, producing frame spikes. At twelve shots a second with multiple weapons and enemies firing too, you would thrash the GC constantly. Object pooling pre-creates a reusable set of objects, hands them out on demand, and recycles them when done — zero allocation during play.

Law.
Anything spawned frequently — bullets, shells, hit sparks, damage numbers, enemies in a horde — should be pooled, not Instantiated/Destroyed at runtime. Pooling is the single biggest GC win in action games.

A bullet pool

Unity ships a built-in ObjectPool<T> (in UnityEngine.Pool) that handles get/release with callbacks. You can also write one in a few lines. Here is a clear hand-rolled pool so you understand the mechanics.

BulletPool.cs
using System.Collections.Generic; using UnityEngine; public class BulletPool : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private Bullet prefab; [SerializeField] private int prewarm = 200; private readonly Queue<Bullet> _idle = new(); void Awake() { for (int i = 0; i < prewarm; i++) _idle.Enqueue(Create()); } private Bullet Create() { Bullet b = Instantiate(prefab, transform); b.SetPool(this); b.gameObject.SetActive(false); return b; } public Bullet Get() { Bullet b = _idle.Count > 0 ? _idle.Dequeue() : Create(); b.gameObject.SetActive(true); return b; } public void Release(Bullet b) { b.gameObject.SetActive(false); _idle.Enqueue(b); // back in the pool, ready to reuse } }
Bullet.cs
using UnityEngine; public class Bullet : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private float speed = 20f; [SerializeField] private float lifetime = 2f; [SerializeField] private int damage = 1; private BulletPool _pool; private Vector2 _dir; private float _age; public void SetPool(BulletPool p) => _pool = p; public void Launch(Vector2 dir) { _dir = dir.normalized; _age = 0f; // reset state on reuse! } void Update() { transform.position += (Vector3)(_dir * speed * Time.deltaTime); _age += Time.deltaTime; if (_age >= lifetime) _pool.Release(this); // recycle, don't Destroy } void OnTriggerEnter2D(Collider2D other) { if (other.TryGetComponent<IDamageable>(out var d)) d.TakeDamage(damage, transform.position); _pool.Release(this); // always return on hit } }
Watch out.
The number-one pooling bug is stale state: a recycled bullet keeps its old velocity, timer, or trail. Always fully reset an object in its Launch/spawn method (age, direction, particle trails). Treat Get() like a constructor.

Notice Bullet hits anything implementing IDamageable — the same interface from your combat lesson. Your enemies, crates, and bosses already work as targets with zero new code. That is interface reuse paying dividends across genres.

🎮 Build This: Twin-Stick Shooter — Bullet Hell

This starts a new genre: the twin-stick / top-down shooter. It reuses the IDamageable interface and Health system from the Metroidvania combat lesson.

  1. Set up Move and Aim Input Actions and a TwinStickController that moves on the left stick and faces the right stick / mouse.
  2. Build a BulletPool that prewarms 200+ bullets and a Bullet that fully resets its state on Launch.
  3. Fire pooled bullets at the chosen fire rate from a muzzle transform; confirm bullets recycle on lifetime and on hit.
  4. Add enemies with Health/IDamageable so bullets damage them through the interface with no new bullet code.
  5. Open the Profiler while firing continuously and confirm GC allocations stay near zero compared to an Instantiate/Destroy version.

title: 3D First-Person Shooter: 3D Physics, Raycasts & Hitscan
html:
Stepping into 3D for real

Time to build a genuine 3D first-person shooter. Everything you know transfers — GameObjects, components, MonoBehaviours, the Animator, Cinemachine, pooling — but now in three dimensions with 3D physics (Rigidbody, Collider, not the 2D variants), mouse-look, and hitscan raycast weapons. This is a new genre that proves Unity is a full 3D engine, not a 2D toy.

3D vs 2D physics

The 3D physics types drop the 2D suffix and add a third axis: Rigidbody, BoxCollider, SphereCollider, CapsuleCollider, MeshCollider, and callbacks OnCollisionEnter / OnTriggerEnter (no 2D). Rotation is a Quaternion over three axes. For a first-person player, the standard body shape is a CapsuleCollider.

First-person controller: look and move

An FPS controller splits into two motions: mouse-look (rotate the body left/right, pitch the camera up/down) and movement (relative to where you face). Lock and hide the cursor for a real FPS feel.

FpsController.cs
using UnityEngine; [RequireComponent(typeof(Rigidbody))] public class FpsController : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private Transform cam; // camera child of player [SerializeField] private float moveSpeed = 6f; [SerializeField] private float mouseSensitivity = 2f; [SerializeField] private float jumpForce = 5f; [SerializeField] private LayerMask groundMask; private Rigidbody _rb; private float _pitch; void Awake() { _rb = GetComponent<Rigidbody>(); _rb.freezeRotation = true; // we rotate manually Cursor.lockState = CursorLockMode.Locked; } void Update() { // --- Mouse look --- float mx = Input.GetAxisRaw("Mouse X") * mouseSensitivity; float my = Input.GetAxisRaw("Mouse Y") * mouseSensitivity; transform.Rotate(Vector3.up * mx); // yaw the body _pitch = Mathf.Clamp(_pitch - my, -85f, 85f); cam.localRotation = Quaternion.Euler(_pitch, 0f, 0f); // pitch camera if (Input.GetButtonDown("Jump") && Grounded()) _rb.AddForce(Vector3.up * jumpForce, ForceMode.Impulse); } void FixedUpdate() { float h = Input.GetAxisRaw("Horizontal"); float v = Input.GetAxisRaw("Vertical"); Vector3 dir = (transform.right * h + transform.forward * v).normalized; Vector3 vel = dir * moveSpeed; vel.y = _rb.velocity.y; // preserve gravity/jump _rb.velocity = vel; } private bool Grounded() => Physics.Raycast(transform.position, Vector3.down, 1.2f, groundMask); }
Tip.
For the camera, parent a Cinemachine POV vcam or a plain Camera to the player. The mouse-look/move split here is the foundation; in the next lesson, enemy AI with NavMesh will hunt this exact controller.

Raycasts: the core of 3D shooting

A raycast shoots an invisible line from a point in a direction and reports the first collider it hits. It is the workhorse of 3D: ground checks, interaction, line-of-sight, and hitscan weapons (instant-hit guns like pistols and rifles, as opposed to slow projectiles). Physics.Raycast fills a RaycastHit with the hit point, normal, distance, and collider.

HitscanWeapon.cs
using UnityEngine; public class HitscanWeapon : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private Camera cam; [SerializeField] private float range = 100f; [SerializeField] private int damage = 25; [SerializeField] private float fireRate = 8f; [SerializeField] private LayerMask hitMask; // what bullets can hit [SerializeField] private GameObject impactVfx; private float _next; void Update() { if (Input.GetButton("Fire1") && Time.time >= _next) { _next = Time.time + 1f / fireRate; Fire(); } } private void Fire() { // Ray from screen center (the crosshair) into the world. Ray ray = cam.ViewportPointToRay(new Vector3(0.5f, 0.5f, 0f)); if (Physics.Raycast(ray, out RaycastHit hit, range, hitMask)) { if (hit.collider.TryGetComponent<IDamageable3D>(out var d)) d.TakeDamage(damage, hit.point); // Spawn impact aligned to the surface normal. if (impactVfx) Instantiate(impactVfx, hit.point, Quaternion.LookRotation(hit.normal)); } } }

Layer masks: control what gets hit

A LayerMask tells a raycast which layers to consider. Put the player on a Player layer and exclude it from the weapon's hitMask so you never shoot yourself; put enemies and world geometry on layers you do include. Layer masks also drive the Physics collision matrix (Project Settings > Physics) so, for example, enemy bullets ignore other enemies. This is essential, performant filtering.

Watch out.
Raycasts only hit objects with colliders, and by default ignore trigger colliders depending on Physics.queriesHitTriggers. If shots pass through an enemy, check that it has a non-trigger collider on a layer included in hitMask. For fast targets, a single ray can tunnel past — use a SphereCast for forgiving aim or a thicker check.

For visible tracers or a shotgun, loop several raycasts with slight spread, or use the pooled-projectile pattern from the twin-stick lesson for slow projectiles. Hitscan (instant) and projectile (travel time) are the two weapon archetypes; real games mix both.

🎮 Build This: First-Person Shooter (3D) — Range Trainer

This starts a new genre: the 3D first-person shooter. The enemies and AI that hunt you will be added in the next (NavMesh) lesson; here you build the controller and weapon.

  1. Make a 3D scene with a floor and walls, a player capsule with a Rigidbody + CapsuleCollider, and a Camera parented to it.
  2. Add FpsController for mouse-look + WASD movement + grounded jump, with the cursor locked.
  3. Create an IDamageable3D interface and put Health-style targets on shootable dummies on an Enemy layer.
  4. Build HitscanWeapon that raycasts from the crosshair, applies damage through the interface, and spawns an impact VFX aligned to the surface normal.
  5. Configure LayerMasks so shots hit world + enemies but never the player, and pool the impact effects to keep firing allocation-free.
